posts.title: Why AFRIVORA Matters
posts.author: Jatto Godswill Osose (Jatto Whisper)
posts.excerpt: Bringing theatre, culture, and commerce together: why AFRIVORA's inaugural edition adapts a play to an unconventional venue, stages Edo language, and pairs performance with a trade fair.
posts.body:
Every established cultural festival began somewhere small: a first edition, a modest venue, and a group of people convinced that a story deserved a permanent home on the calendar. On 10 July 2026, AFRIVORA begins that journey at the YWCA Hall, Airport Road, Benin City. Its inaugural edition brings together a stage play, a cultural festival, and a trade fair in a single experience, with the ambition of becoming an annual fixture every July.

The question worth asking isn't just what AFRIVORA is. It's why it should exist at all. The answer lies in how this inaugural edition brings together three complementary ideas: a stage play adapted to an unconventional performance space, the vitality of indigenous language in contemporary theatre, and a marketplace that recognises culture as both heritage and enterprise.

A Play, Adapted

This year's featured production, NEKIGHIDI, written by Ossa Earliece, is staged at the YWCA Hall rather than a conventional theatre. That choice belongs to this edition specifically, not to some fixed rule about how AFRIVORA must always work. Future editions may be presented in purpose-built theatres or other performance venues, depending on the artistic and production needs of each edition. What matters this year is the adaptation itself: taking a play originally conceived for a conventional theatre and reimagining it within a community hall that was never designed as a performance venue.

British theatre director Peter Brook opened his book The Empty Space with the idea that a man crossing an empty space while someone else watches is, in itself, enough for an act of theatre to happen. His real target was what he called Deadly Theatre, theatre that had become routine rather than alive, so dependent on convention and infrastructure that the living encounter between performer and audience gets buried under it. Adapting NEKIGHIDI to a community hall is therefore less a rejection of the conventional theatre than an exploration of theatre's remarkable adaptability.

A Language Carries More Than Words

NEKIGHIDI is performed partly in Edo, as an effort to preserve and strengthen the public use of the language. Language is more than vocabulary. It carries memory, humour, worldview, history, philosophy, and the accumulated experiences of a people. When a language disappears from performance, something deeper than words is lost with it.

Putting Edo on stage, alongside movement, rhythm, and gesture the way older African storytelling traditions have always used them, is a way of insisting that the language still belongs in contemporary art, not only in memory. A viewer doesn't need to understand every word to feel what the language is carrying.

Where Commerce and Culture Meet

Across much of Africa, festivals were rarely just artistic events. They were spaces where commerce, politics, religion, and performance happened at the same time, in the same place, often among the same people.

"A festival wasn't a break from ordinary life; it was ordinary life, intensified."

AFRIVORA's Trade Fair picks up that same thread. In that sense, the Trade Fair isn't peripheral to AFRIVORA. It's one of the ways the festival insists that culture must remain economically sustainable if it is to remain socially relevant. Local artisans, entrepreneurs, and vendors become active participants in the festival rather than observers of it.

An Initiative of AFRIN Arts

AFRIVORA is an initiative of AFRIN Arts, a creative organisation committed to producing theatre and cultural experiences rooted in African heritage. Through performance, festivals, and artistic collaboration, AFRIN Arts seeks to preserve cultural memory while creating opportunities for contemporary creative expression and enterprise. For AFRIN Arts, performance is not only an artistic practice but also a means of education, cultural preservation, and community engagement. Conceived as an annual festival, AFRIVORA reflects that broader vision.

Why the First Edition Matters

Festivals don't become traditions by announcing that they will. They become traditions by returning, year after year, until the date itself carries meaning. Every established cultural festival alive today began with a first edition, a small group of people betting that a story was worth an annual home. AFRIVORA begins with that same bet.

Whether AFRIVORA becomes an enduring tradition will depend not only on its organisers but also on the community that embraces it. Festivals are sustained by artists who create, audiences who attend, businesses that invest, and communities that believe culture deserves a permanent place in public life.

"Culture survives not because it is remembered, but because it is practised."

Every lasting tradition begins with people willing to show up for the first chapter.

The first edition is always the hardest.

It is also the one history remembers.
